filer: National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners
overall_summary: NARUC argues that jurisdiction over retail load interconnection belongs exclusively to the states under the Federal Power Act and that FERC must affirmatively disclaim any assertion of that authority. While opposing federal encroachment, NARUC pledges cooperative engagement to find legally durable solutions that connect large loads without harming existing retail customers or displacing state tools like large load tariffs and resource planning.

quotes.text: It is imperative that FERC, in any final rulemaking, make clear that it is affirmatively not asserting jurisdiction over these facilities, or end-use sales which fall squarely within the exclusive jurisdiction of state energy regulatory authorities.
quotes.concern: FERC must explicitly disclaim jurisdiction over distribution interconnections, generation, and retail sales

quotes.text: the Commission should avoid any action that would circumvent or negate state decisions governing the provision of retail service, including, but not limited to, determinations about contracting practices related to the assessment of generating costs to retail load and the permissibility of direct retail access or retail wheeling.
quotes.concern: FERC action must not override state decisions on retail contracting, cost assessment, or retail wheeling

quotes.text: FERC has never attempted to assert jurisdiction over end-user load interconnections. The reason for this fact is that FERC asserting jurisdiction over load interconnection is outside the boundaries imposed by the FPA.
quotes.concern: FERC has historically not claimed jurisdiction over load interconnection because the FPA does not permit it

quotes.text: Section 201(b) of the FPA grants the states exclusive jurisdiction "over facilities used for the generation of electric energy or over facilities used in local distribution or for the transmission of electric energy in intrastate commerce, or over facilities for the transmission of electric energy consumed wholly by the transmitter."
quotes.concern: FPA section 201(b) reserves to states exclusive authority over local distribution and intrastate transmission facilities

quotes.text: If FERC were to assert jurisdiction over a service to a limited class of retail customers, it would interfere with the balancing performed by state regulators in retail rate cases, a determination that is solely a state decision, and which has significant rate impacts on all classes of customers.
quotes.concern: Federal jurisdiction over a subset of retail customers would disrupt state rate-balancing across all customer classes

quotes.text: Jurisdiction over large load interconnection falls squarely within the residual intrastate oversight originally undertaken by states and continuously exercised thereafter.
quotes.concern: Large load interconnection is a traditional state function that has never been federalized

quotes.text: State public utility commissions retain jurisdiction over retail load interconnections, regardless of size, however, it is also true that the United States and its citizens will benefit from interconnecting large loads in a timely and non-discriminatory manner.
quotes.concern: States hold jurisdiction over retail load interconnection regardless of load size, while acknowledging national interest in timely connection

quotes.text: NARUC pledges to engage with regulated entities and other stakeholders to explore consensus solutions for FERC's consideration that will help meet national goals for large load interconnection, while avoiding disputes over jurisdiction that would impede achieving our shared goals.
quotes.concern: NARUC commits to collaborative engagement to find solutions that serve national interconnection goals without jurisdictional conflict

quotes.text: any large load interconnections without sufficient available generation capacity could threaten reliable power service to existing retail customers, as grid operators may lack the resources needed to maintain system stability during peak demand or extreme weather events
quotes.concern: Rapid large load interconnection without adequate generation capacity poses reliability risks to existing retail customers

quotes.text: the costs of large load interconnections, including necessary transmission upgrades and other infrastructure investments, may unfairly burden retail ratepayers if not properly allocated
quotes.concern: Interconnection costs must be allocated fairly to avoid shifting burdens onto retail ratepayers

quotes.text: at least 20 states have approved or have pending large load tariffs or similar measures, which may include financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms to allow for the rapid interconnection of large loads without compromising grid reliability or unduly burdening existing retail customers
quotes.concern: Existing state large load tariffs already address rapid interconnection with reliability and cost protections

quotes.text: NARUC respectfully requests that the FERC bring federal and state regulators together for a detailed examination of the challenges and goals.
quotes.concern: NARUC requests a joint federal-state forum to examine large load interconnection challenges

bins.name: State jurisdiction over retail interconnection
bins.description: NARUC argues that the FPA exclusively reserves jurisdiction over retail load interconnection to the states, regardless of load size. FERC has never asserted this jurisdiction and the FPA bars it from doing so. Any final rule must affirmatively disclaim authority over distribution interconnections, generation, and retail sales, and must not override state decisions on contracting or retail wheeling.

bins.name: Fair cost allocation to retail customers
bins.description: NARUC supports ensuring that interconnection costs, including transmission upgrades, are allocated fairly and do not impose undue burdens on existing retail ratepayers. Federal assertion of jurisdiction over a subset of retail customers would disrupt state rate-balancing across all customer classes.

bins.name: State flexibility tools should be preserved
bins.description: At least 20 states have approved or pending large load tariffs with financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms. NARUC supports preserving these state flexibility mechanisms rather than displacing them with federal rulemaking.

bins.name: Preserve state retail regulatory authority
bins.description: NARUC emphasizes that state commissions must retain authority over retail contracting practices, cost assessment to retail load, and determinations about retail access. Federal action that circumvents these state decisions would harm all customer classes, not only large loads.

bins.name: FPA statutory limits on FERC authority
bins.description: NARUC cites the specific text of FPA section 201(b) to establish that states hold exclusive jurisdiction over local distribution, intrastate transmission, and generation facilities. This statutory reservation forms the legal foundation for NARUC's jurisdictional arguments.

bins.name: Cooperative federal-state engagement
bins.description: While opposing federal encroachment, NARUC pledges to work collaboratively with FERC and stakeholders toward consensus solutions. NARUC requests that FERC convene a joint federal-state forum to examine challenges and goals before issuing a final rule.

bins.name: Reliability risk to existing customers
bins.description: NARUC warns that connecting large loads without sufficient generation capacity could threaten reliable service to existing retail customers during peak demand or extreme weather events. This concern supports the case for careful state-level oversight of interconnection decisions.

bins.name: Interconnection cost burden on ratepayers
bins.description: NARUC flags that transmission upgrade costs and other infrastructure investments tied to large load interconnections may unfairly shift onto retail ratepayers if not properly allocated, reinforcing the need for state commission oversight.

bins.name: Existing state large load tariff programs
bins.description: NARUC notes that at least 20 states already have approved or pending large load tariffs covering financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms. These programs demonstrate that states are actively managing large load interconnection without federal intervention.
